Show me an example of channel/new in Sema.

;; A producer feeds a bounded queue; the consumer drains it until close.
(define ch (channel/new 2))
(async (for-each (fn (n) (channel/send ch n)) '(1 2 3))
       (channel/close ch))
(define (drain acc)
  (let ((v (channel/recv ch)))
    (if (null? v) (reverse acc) (drain (cons v acc)))))
(await (async (drain '())))  ; => (1 2 3)

Write Sema code: Worker: pull jobs until the channel is drained+closed; "process" each (here: square it, with a tiny sleep to force interleaving) and emit a result. got a job nothing buffered but channel still open — yield and retry drained and closed — done

(define (make-worker id)
  (async
    (let ((more #t))
      (while more
        (let ((job (channel/try-recv jobs)))
          (cond
            
            ((not (nil? job))
             (async/sleep 1)
             (channel/send results {:worker id :job job :result (* job job)}))
            
            ((not (channel/closed? jobs)) (async/sleep 0))
            
            (else (set! more #f))))))))

Show me an example of gensym in Sema.

(symbol? (gensym))      ; => #t
(= (gensym) (gensym))   ; => #f  (every call is distinct)

;; A swap macro that needs a temporary — gensym keeps it from
;; capturing a variable the caller might also be named.
(defmacro swap2 (a b)
  (let ((tmp (gensym "tmp")))
    (list 'let (list (list tmp a))
          (list 'set! a b)
          (list 'set! b tmp))))

(define x 1)
(define y 2)
(swap2 x y)
(list x y)   ; => (2 1)

Write Sema code: A tiny bytecode compiler + virtual machine, in ~80 lines of Sema. Compiles s-expressions like (+ 1 (* 2 3)) and (if ...) into a flat list of stack-machine instructions, then runs them in a dispatch loop — the same shape as Sema's own engine, just smaller. The "magic" of compilers turns out to be a recursive walk that emits operands before operators, a couple of jumps for control flow, and a loop with a `cond` in it. Full walkthrough: https://sema-lang.com/docs/internals/build-a-bytecode-vm.html --- tiny helpers ---
(define (second xs) (nth xs 1))

  (let ((ix (int (round (* x XS)))) (iy (int (round y))))

        (assoc canvas (+ (* iy W) ix) ch)
        canvas)))

Write Sema code: Conditional thread-first: short-circuits on nil

(defmacro some-> (val . forms)
  (if (null? forms)
    val
    (let ((form (car forms))
          (rest (cdr forms)))
      (if (list? form)
        `(let ((__v ,val))
          (if (nil? __v) nil (some-> (,(car form) __v ,@(cdr form)) ,@rest)))
        `(let ((__v ,val))
          (if (nil? __v) nil (some-> (,form __v) ,@rest)))))))

Write Sema code: multimethods.sema — Type-dispatched polymorphism Multimethods dispatch on a value derived from arguments, allowing open extension without modifying existing code. Uses maps as dispatch tables and closures for encapsulation. Demonstrates: closures as objects, maps as dispatch tables, type, dynamic dispatch, keyword dispatch --- Threading macro (needed for builder chains) ---

(defmacro -> (val . forms)
  (if (null? forms)
    val
    (let ((form (car forms))
          (rest (cdr forms)))
      (if (list? form)
        `(-> (,(car form) ,val ,@(cdr form)) ,@rest)
        `(-> (,form ,val) ,@rest)))))

(defmulti fizzbuzz (fn (n)
  (cond ((= (modulo n 15) 0) :fizzbuzz)
        ((= (modulo n 3) 0)  :fizz)
        ((= (modulo n 5) 0)  :buzz)
        (#t                  :num))))

(defmethod fizzbuzz :fizzbuzz (fn (n) "FizzBuzz"))
(defmethod fizzbuzz :fizz     (fn (n) "Fizz"))
(defmethod fizzbuzz :buzz     (fn (n) "Buzz"))
(defmethod fizzbuzz :num      (fn (n) n))

(fizzbuzz 7)   ; => 7

Write Sema code: Generic stage: for each value in `in`, send (f value) to `out`; close `out` when `in` is drained and closed. wait for more

(define (stage in out f)
  (async
    (let ((more #t))
      (while more
        (let ((v (channel/try-recv in)))
          (cond
            ((not (nil? v)) (channel/send out (f v)))
            ((not (channel/closed? in)) (async/sleep 0))   
            (else (channel/close out) (set! more #f))))))))

Write Sema code: Quicksort

(define (qsort lst)
  (if (or (null? lst) (null? (cdr lst)))
      lst
      (let ((pivot (car lst))
            (rest  (cdr lst)))
        (append
          (qsort (filter (fn (x) (< x pivot)) rest))
          (list pivot)
          (qsort (filter (fn (x) (>= x pivot)) rest))))))

Write Sema code: List and map comprehensions via macros Implements Python/Haskell-style comprehensions: (for/list ((x xs) (y ys)) body) (for/list ((x xs) (:when pred)) body) Simple list comprehension

(defmacro for/list (bindings body)
  (define (expand bs)
    (if (null? bs)
      `(list ,body)
      (let ((clause (car bs))
            (rest (cdr bs)))
        (if (and (list? clause) (= (car clause) :when))
          `(let ((__result ,(expand rest)))
             (if ,(cadr clause) __result '()))
          (let ((var (car clause))
                (seq (cadr clause)))
            `(apply append
               (map (fn (,var) ,(expand rest)) ,seq)))))))
  (expand bindings))

Write Sema code: comprehensions.sema — List and map comprehensions via macros Implements Python/Haskell-style comprehensions: (for/list ((x xs) (y ys)) body) (for/list ((x xs) (:when pred)) body) Demonstrates: nested macros, map/filter composition, quasiquote --- Comprehension Macros --- Simple list comprehension: (for/list ((var seq) ...) body) Supports (:when pred) clauses for filtering All bindings are expanded at macro time (no runtime macro calls), so the generated code works with the stdlib's HOF mini-evaluator. Filter clause: (:when pred) Binding clause: (var seq)

(defmacro for/list (bindings body)
  (define (expand bs)
    (if (null? bs)
      `(list ,body)
      (let ((clause  (car bs))
            (rest    (cdr bs)))
        (if (and (list? clause) (= (car clause) :when))
          
          `(let ((__result ,(expand rest)))
            (if ,(cadr clause) __result '()))
          
          (let ((var (car clause))
                (seq (cadr clause)))
            `(apply append
              (map (fn (,var) ,(expand rest)) ,seq)))))))
  (expand bindings))

;; Inside a task a full channel suspends the sender until the consumer catches up.
;; Capacity 1, two sends, two receives — the second send yields until the first
;; value is taken.
(define ch (channel/new 1))
(define producer
  (async (channel/send ch :a)    ; fits
         (channel/send ch :b)))  ; full → yields here
(await (async (list (channel/recv ch) (channel/recv ch))))  ; => (:a :b)

Write Sema code: Fan-in — many producers, one channel, one collector. sensor-A ─┐ sensor-B ─┼─▶ [readings] ─▶ collector ─▶ dashboard sensor-C ─┘ Several "sensors" run concurrently and push readings into ONE shared channel; a single collector drains them. This is the many-to-one merge — the inverse of fan-out. Each sensor yields (async/sleep 0) between readings so control passes around the pool. The merge order below is whatever the cooperative scheduler produced — fully deterministic (it's identical every run), and the collector safely receives all readings from every producer no matter how they interleave.

Write Sema code: sample points along the segment (x0,y0)->(x1,y1) so the line has no gaps

(define (draw-line canvas x0 y0 x1 y1 ch)
  (let* ((dx (- x1 x0)) (dy (- y1 y0))
         (steps (max 1 (int (round (* 2.0 (max (abs (* dx XS)) (abs dy))))))))
    (let loop ((i 0) (c canvas))
      (if (> i steps) c
          (let ((tt (/ i (* 1.0 steps))))
            (loop (+ i 1) (plot c (+ x0 (* tt dx)) (+ y0 (* tt dy)) ch)))))))

Write Sema code: math-and-crypto.sema — Math, crypto, base64, UUID, bitwise operations Demonstrates: math/*, bit/*, uuid, base64, hash, time --- Prime sieve ---

(define (sieve-primes n)
  (let loop ((candidates (range 2 n)) (primes '()))
    (if (null? candidates)
      (reverse primes)
      (let ((p (first candidates)))
        (loop
          (filter (fn (x) (not (= 0 (math/remainder x p)))) (rest candidates))
          (cons p primes))))))

(define (next-cell grid r c)
  (let* ((alive (grid-get grid r c))
         (n (count-neighbors grid r c)))
    (cond ((and (= alive 1) (< n 2)) 0)
          ((and (= alive 1) (> n 3)) 0)
          ((and (= alive 0) (= n 3)) 1)
          (else alive))))

(foldr - 0 '(1 2 3))                               ; => 2

  (define (go a b i)
